text:
Lee Raymond, the oil executive who created Exxon Mobil via a mega-merger in 1998 and for many years fought global efforts to reduce CO2 emissions, has died. He was 87. 

Raymond ran Exxon from 1993, and negotiated the merger deal with Mobil CEO Lucio A. Noto, emerging as CEO of the combined entity. Exxon Mobil soon after became the world’s largest publicly traded company as oil prices surged.

text: U.S./IRAN try to maximise leverage in negotiations while avoiding uncontrolled escalation: https://t.co/J9qzmflbvw

Oil Prices Slide as Traders Prepare for Blockade to End

Crude oil prices are falling, with the sharpest declines in the spot market and near-dated futures contracts, even as global petroleum inventories deplete rapidly to the lowest for the time of year since 2008.

The entire forward pricing curve has flattened significantly since the end of April after steepening in the first two months of the war between the United States and Iran.

The speed and scale of the depletion would ordinarily have resulted in a much bigger rise in spot and near-dated futures contracts and a fiercer backwardation.

But traders have become increasingly convinced tanker traffic through the Strait will resume soon, either as a result of a diplomatic agreement or increased military protection of vessels by the United States ...

text:
Sure, this could just be another one of Trump’s endless bluffs—I get that. But there’s another thing I know for a fact.

If this is just an operational play to force a deal, it’s actually going to push the very deal Washington wants further out of reach, unless they either completely flatten Iran or force a regime change.

The current Iranian leadership is not going to sign a thing just bc Trump is making threats. And unless they are physically wiped out or totally dismantled into submission, dropping bombs isn't going to make them sign anything either.

If anything, it just drives Iran further away from the negotiating table. On top of that, the odds of Iran launching an even more indiscriminate retaliation than before the ceasefire are way too high. 

The US either need to go all the way, or not do it at all.

#oott #iran
It looks highly likely I’ll be forced into working overtime again today..

If you aggregate all the Middle Eastern crude flows cutting through the SoH since the April ceasefire, you do get a print right around 100 mb. 

But let's be real, that entire tanker fleet wasn't riding with a US Navy escort.

Highlights from EIAs weekly report:
Crude stocks fell for a seventh straight week by 7.2mb to a 2022 seasonal low. 
Cushing fell to 21.6 mb, near but still above operational minimum levels around 20 mb
SPR inventories fell 7.9 mb, holding just above the 40-year low from 2023
Production saw a meaningful increase of 92 kb/d to 13.8 mb/d  
Crude export slowed 1mb/d to 4.8 mb/d, with total crude and fuel exports falling to nine-week low at 12.5 mb/d.
Refinery utilisation held at a 2018 high just over 95%, with jet fuel production hitting a fresh record
Diesel stockpiles slumped to a 1990 seasonal low
Implied demand for gasoline (4-wk avg.) held steady while distillate rose 109 kb/d

US CPI came in broadly in line with expectations with the negative supply chock lifting headline inflation to 4.2%, its highest level in three years, while core inflation edged up to 2.9%. The figures underpins the market's current focus on persistent inflation risks, driven by higher energy prices, and the prospect of higher-for-longer interest rates. 
On a 3-month annualised based the headline #inflation reached 8%.

@MarioNawfal Significant Demand Destruction: Wall Street banks like Goldman Sachs report that high prices and flight cancellations have already destroyed 4 to 5 million barrels per day of global oil demand. Both OPEC and the International Energy Agency (IEA) have slashed the expectations

ANOTHER HAPPENING DAY IN THE MARKET

1) Markets sold off sharply on Wednesday, with the Dow dropping 953 points (-1.87%) to close at 49,918.78, the S&P 500 falling 1.62% to 7,267.65, and the Nasdaq shedding 1.98% to close at 25,169.50. The twin catalysts were a hot May CPI print and a fresh escalation in the Middle East, with President Trump threatening new strikes on Iran after Tehran shot down a U.S. Apache helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz.

2) May CPI came in at 4.2% year-over-year, the highest inflation reading in three years, with motor fuel prices up 41% compared to a year ago. Energy alone accounted for over 60% of the monthly CPI increase. Core CPI (ex-food and energy) rose 0.2% month-over-month and 2.9% year-over-year, which was broadly in line with estimates. Markets are now pricing a 25-bps rate hike before year-end, with Deutsche Bank noting that the Fed is increasingly focused on inflation after three straight above-consensus jobs reports.

3) $SMCI was the session's biggest large-cap collapse, plunging 27.98% to $29.27 on 186.3M shares (market cap ~$17.6B) after the AI server manufacturer announced plans to raise $7 billion through equity and equity-linked financing transactions to fund component purchases needed to satisfy roughly $39 billion in AI server orders. Investors punished the stock on dilution fears, with Seeking Alpha calling the move a forced sale driven by reputational risk and balance sheet concerns.

4) Oil surged as geopolitical tensions flared, with Brent crude closing above $93.10 per barrel, up 1.8% on the day, as President Trump threatened additional strikes on Iran and Iran signaled it could halt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. WTI crude tracked higher as well, climbing nearly 2% in the session. The $USO ETF gained 2.28%, while airline stocks buckled under the fuel-cost implications.

5) Airlines were among the hardest hit sectors as oil spiked: $UAL fell 6.25% to $102.78, $DAL slid 5.79% to $76.47, and $AAL dropped 4.76% to $13.42 on 136.3M shares. Seeking Alpha noted the selloff was driven by fears of sharply rising jet fuel costs as Middle East tensions threatened sustained elevated oil prices, hitting carriers with minimal fuel hedging.

6) The semiconductor sector continued its painful rotation lower, with the chip sell-off accelerating after Broadcom's disappointing revenue forecast last week. $AVGO fell 5.12% to $372.10 on 37.4M shares (market cap ~$1.76T), $AMD dropped 4.86% to $452.40 on 27.2M shares, $MU slid 4.70% to $891.88 on 54.0M shares, and $NVDA lost 3.73% to $200.42 on 156.7M shares. Reuters flagged the broader narrative as "Sizzling AI now fizzling," with investors increasingly concerned about stretched AI valuations ahead of major IPOs including SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI.

7) $CBRL Cracker Barrel was a rare bright spot, surging 22.56% to $44.49 on 11.3M shares after reporting Q3 fiscal 2026 earnings that blew past estimates: EPS of $0.29 vs. analyst consensus of negative $0.48, and revenue of $797.4M vs. $776.7M expected. The company also raised its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance to $120M-$125M and raised full-year revenue guidance, signaling the logo controversy that plagued the brand in 2025 is firmly in the rearview mirror.

8) $ORCL Oracle reported Q4 fiscal 2026 results after the close: EPS of $2.11 vs. $1.96 estimated (8% beat), and revenue of $19.18B vs. $19.1B expected, up 21% year-over-year. The company raised its adjusted EPS outlook for the year and reiterated its $90B revenue target for fiscal 2027. However, shares fell 7% in after-hours trading as investors focused on capital expenditures surging to $55.66B, a 162% increase, stoking concerns that AI data center buildout costs are ballooning faster than cloud revenue can support them.

9) Cantor Fitzgerald raised price targets across the semiconductor equipment complex in a sweeping note, lifting $KLAC to $2,500 from $2,000, $LRCX to $425 from $320, and $AMAT to $650 from $575, citing sustained robust investment in wafer fabrication equipment and continued AI-driven demand for advanced chip tooling. Seeking Alpha reported the note, with Cantor adjusting its CY26 WFE forecast upward. Despite the upgrades, KLAC slipped 0.17%, LRCX fell 1.64%, and AMAT edged down 0.44%, as the broader tech selloff offset the analyst tailwind.

10) Daiwa more than doubled its price target on $MU Micron Technology to $1,600 from $700, one of the largest single-day price target raises in the semiconductor space in recent memory. Daiwa cited the company's upcoming fiscal Q3 report on June 24, where consensus is modeling $33.5B in revenue with an 81% gross margin, reflecting what the firm sees as a structural shift in the memory market driven by AI-related HBM demand. MU closed at $891.88, implying roughly 79% upside to the new target.

11) $HOOD Robinhood rose 3.09% to $86.36 on 41.3M shares, bucking the market decline. Bloomberg reported that Cantor Fitzgerald raised its price target to $110 from $100, citing upside from prediction markets as Robinhood's Rothera joint venture captures the majority of exchange economics from its growing prediction markets volumes. Goldman Sachs also raised its target to $108 from $105. A Barron's report noted that a director bought roughly $35M of stock last week via a venture fund.

12) The top 10 most active options today by contracts traded were $TSLA with 3.67M contracts, $NVDA with 3.23M contracts, $AAPL with 1.74M contracts, $AMZN with 821K contracts, $SMCI with 696K contracts, $MSFT with 605K contracts, $META with 579K contracts, $ORCL with 569K contracts, $MU with 530K contracts, and $HOOD with 506K contracts. SMCI's activity ran 229% of its daily average, and HOOD ran 154% of average. The session's most-traded single contract was the NVDA Jun 10 $200 put with 335K contracts, a bearish bet on Nvidia closing exactly at or below the psychologically significant $200 level.

13) $CAVA surged 6.92% to $81.56 (market cap ~$9.5B) after UBS upgraded the Mediterranean fast-casual chain to Buy from Neutral, calling for a breakout in unit economics and comparable store sales as the brand expands nationally. UBS did not issue a specific price target in the publicly available note, but the upgrade bucked a session where Consumer Cyclical as a whole fell 1.93%.

14) Defensive stocks were the day's standouts: Consumer Defensive gained 0.62% and Financial Services rose 0.38%, the only two S&P sectors to close in the green. $KO Coca-Cola rose 2.77% to $83.59, hitting an all-time high during the session, as Morgan Stanley reiterated Overweight and identified it as a top pick, citing sustained outperformance driven by its Fairlife milk brand expansion. $TJX jumped 1.69% to $167.66, also setting a fresh record high amid a flight to defensive retail.

Here are the latest stock market updates. Markets remain on edge as tech volatility and Middle East tensions overshadow an in‑line inflation report.

Market Wrap: Tech Drags Indexes Lower

Wall Street extended declines on Wednesday as technology stocks remained under pressure and renewed US‑Iran tensions overshadowed a tame inflation reading. 

The Nasdaq Composite lost 1.18% to 25,375, while the S&P 500 slipped 0.74% to 7,349 (unofficial). The Dow Jones Industrial Average, buoyed by a defensive rotation, managed a 0.20% gain to close around 50,877.

European and Asian markets also felt the weight: 
• Germany's DAX lost 0.97% and 

• France's CAC 40 fell 0.51%, while 

• Japan's Nikkei fell more than 2% amid a global flight from risk assets.

Key Drivers

Geopolitics: US forces launched self‑defense strikes against Iran at President Trump's direction, after Iran shot down a US Apache helicopter over the Strait of Hormuz. 

The strait remains partially blocked, keeping ~20% of global oil supply at risk.

Fed outlook: Goldman Sachs has abandoned its forecast for a Fed rate cut in December 2026, now expecting two quarter‑point cuts only in June and December 2027.

Individual Stock Movers

Super Micro Computer ($SMCI ) fell as much as 19% after unveiling a $7 billion equity raise to fund AI server components, raising dilution concerns after the stock had gained 39% YTD.

Lakeland Industries ($LAKE ) rose 15% on earnings. Cracker Barrel (CBRL) added 8%. Fuller Smith & Turner jumped 8.5% after a top‑and‑bottom‑line beat.

Nvidia ($NVDA ) fell over 2%, extending a 5‑day chip sell‑off that erased $1.4 trillion from semiconductor market caps.

IPO Market Heats Up

SpaceX saw demand four times its target raise, attracting over $250 billion in investor interest for its Friday Nasdaq debut.

OpenAI confidentially filed for a US IPO, joining rival Anthropic in pushing toward public markets to tap surging AI investor demand.

GOLD JUST LOST $1.1 TRILLION IN A SINGLE DAY

The asset everyone called a safe haven just had one of its worst sessions of 2026.

- Gold peaked at $5,418 per ounce in january an all time high
- It is now trading at $4,174 a 23% crash from that peak
- The May job report added 172,000 jobs vs 85,000 expected almost double forecasts
- That blowout print pushed Fed rate hike odds to 70% by December
- Goldman Sachs now expects the first Fed cut in 2027 not 2026

The Iran war was supposed to make gold the safest place on earth

instead it made oil expensive inflation worse and Fed more aggressive.

Gold didn't see that coming Neither did $1.1 trillion that just evaporated.

The report I published yesterday, when fear was dominant again and everything felt dark. Enjoy the read 👇
June 10, 2026 US Consumer Price Index CPI Report

MarketMaestro

21 hours ago
The market is locked onto Consumer Price Index, CPI, data. This data set is seen as the most critical macroeconomic crossroads that will determine market direction on the eve of the first Federal Open Market Committee, FOMC, meeting under new Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, amid the energy shocks created by escalating geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East, new tariff walls in supply chains, and unprecedented structural cost increases created by artificial intelligence investments.

General Consensus and Expectation Ranges

According to analyst estimates collected by FactSet, headline CPI is expected to rise 0.5% month over month in May and climb from the previous month’s 3.8% level to 4.2% year over year. Core CPI, which excludes volatile items such as food and energy and is the main focus of monetary policy, is expected to rise 0.3% month over month and reach 2.9% year over year.

Goldman Sachs: The institution, which has largely abandoned rate cut expectations because of labor market resilience, expects Core Personal Consumption Expenditures, PCE, inflation to gradually fall to 2.1% by December.

JPMorgan Chase: The institution’s Chief US Economist Michael Feroli draws attention to the fragile impact of the May CPI data on market psychology. According to Feroli, an upside inflation surprise above expectations would completely shatter the inflation is transitory narrative in markets and pull forward the timing of the first rate hike. JPMorgan’s models show that in such an inflationary scenario, the probability of the Fed hiking rates by September 2026 would exceed 50%, and that a second hike would start to be priced by December. I have seen this institution be wrong many times.

Morgan Stanley: Matt Hornbach says the May CPI data to be released will be spicier, and that the pressure created by high energy and AI infrastructure costs will keep inflation alive. On the other hand, Chief Global Economist Seth Carpenter expects global growth to be 3.2% in 2026 and inflationary pressures to soften as oil prices decline toward $90 by year end.

Deutsche Bank: The bank offers one of the most aggressive headline inflation estimates on Wall Street, 4.29%, based on its projection of a sharp 6.8% monthly increase in gasoline prices specifically for CPI.

Citi and Barclays: Citi analysts confirm that headline inflation will jump to 4.2%, while saying the real key question is whether there will be a month over month slowdown in core CPI.

Real time market data, platforms such as Zillow, and independent rent indexes show that new rent increases across the US have largely stopped, and that real rent costs are easing as housing supply increases and landlords activate aggressive incentives such as free months to find tenants. However, the survey based OER calculation method used by the BLS follows market realities with a 6 to 12 month lag. As Jim Bianco from Bianco Research also noted, a statistical anomaly caused by the government shutdown threat last September continues to artificially inflate shelter figures. This methodological lag will continue to cause shelter inflation to remain high on paper in the May CPI report, despite cooling in the real economy.

For the May CPI to be released in June, the T-1 signal in Truflation systems produced warnings before the official print, indicating that live market pricing in key components points to upward pressure and that the official print is likely to surprise. However, Truflation Chief Data Officer Oliver Rust says: High figures from the BLS are entirely due to instant jumps in energy and methodological statistical errors in shelter; despite the strong surface appearance in the labor market, the underlying core economy is actually softening rapidly.

Kevin Warsh will chair the first FOMC meeting of his career on June 16-17.

Warsh is expected to completely remove the rate cut friendly bias toward easing from the official statement and shift to a neutral stance policy position. The new Fed Chair is expected to adopt a strategy of keeping rates high for longer by citing stubborn CPI data, in order not to repeat the past inflation is transitory mistake.

Market Scenarios After the Data

If headline inflation approaches Deutsche Bank’s extreme estimates and pushes toward 4.3%, and especially if core goods, because of tariffs, and shelter, because of lagged OER effects, surprise to the upside, the initial market reaction could be extremely destructive.

If the data comes exactly in line with the FactSet median and Bank of America estimates, markets will quickly calm down after the data volatility and shift focus to the FOMC meeting one week later and Kevin Warsh’s communication.

If headline data falls below 4.0% and core inflation records an increase lower than 0.2%, this could create excitement in markets. But this probability currently looks difficult.

The Cleveland Fed’s dynamic model expects headline inflation at 4.05%, below consensus, and projects a more stable path for core inflation at 0.23% month over month and 2.83% year over year. On the other hand, Truflation’s real time data fell to a low level of 1.83%. They see OER and shelter items in BLS data as structural noise.

🦇Batman Rational Forecast for May 2026 CPI

• Headline CPI, monthly: 0.38% to 0.42% range. Direct reflections of the geopolitical energy shock will be effective, but consensus seems overly pessimistic.

• Headline CPI, yearly: 4.0% to 4.1% range. Slightly below the market consensus of 4.2%, closer to the Cleveland Fed’s 4.05% expectation.

• Core CPI, monthly: 0.20% to 0.25% range.

• Core CPI, yearly: flat around 2.8%.

Ultimately, although the data that will hit screens on the morning of June 10 will look uncomfortable at the headline level because of high energy costs and lagged shelter effects, when looking into core details, it may carry signs of structural cooling. The new Federal Reserve leadership under Kevin Warsh will have to take this geopolitical noise in headline figures into account while building its communication strategy, but in the background, it will be aware that restrictive monetary policy is creating a disinflationary gravitational pull in real consumer demand.

In this scenario, if the market chooses tomorrow to strip out the energy and shelter noise in the headline and buy the cooling in the core, we may see a controlled upward move. However, a print at or above the consensus boundary could send hedging costs in the market flying ahead of Kevin Warsh. Because since this is the new Fed Chair’s first meeting, nobody wants to take risk in this uncertainty.

J.P. Morgan's Nifty 2026 Outlook: The Full Picture - 

Several reports are highlighting J.P. Morgan's 20500 Nifty target, creating unnecessary #panic. But here's what the report actually says -

Revised Nifty 50 Targets ( Year End 2026 )

Bull Case: 30000 ( Earlier: 33000 )

Base Case: 27000 ( Earlier: 30000 )

Bear Case: 20500 ( Earlier: 24000 )

The widely discussed 20500 target is not J.P. Morgan's primary forecast. It represents a #worst case scenario driven by -

A prolonged oil price shock

Disruptions in the Strait of #Hormuz

Earnings downgrade across sectors

Weakening market sentiment

Why did J.P. Morgan turn #cautious ?

Indian equities continue to trade at a premium to Emerging Markets.

Earnings growth expectations have been lowered.

Higher crude oil prices could hurt margins and consumption.

Limited #AI and #semiconductor exposure in Indian large caps.

Heavy supply from #IPOs, QIPs, and #promoter stake sales.

Bottom Line - 

J.P. Morgan's base case remains 27000, while 20500 is merely a downside risk scenario. Despite near-term concerns, the firm continues to believe in India's long-term structural growth story.

Bear Case ≠ Base Case.

#Nifty50 #IndianMarkets #StockMarket #Investing #JPMorgan

BREAKING: Oil Prices Soar as US Strikes Iran for Second Day

Crude oil prices rose above $92 a barrel on Thursday, extending gains from the previous session as the US military launched strikes against Iran for a second day, raising concerns that peace efforts could collapse and the conflict disrupting global markets could drag on.

President Donald Trump accused Tehran of obstructing negotiations on an interim peace deal, while Tehran said it would stand firm against any threats.

Earlier this week, the US launched what it described as "self-defense strikes" in response to the downing of an American helicopter, with Iran retaliating by targeting US military facilities in Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait.

The near-total closure of the Strait of Hormuz continues to tighten global energy supplies, although there are indications that limited oil volumes are still leaving the Persian Gulf. Meanwhile, EIA data showed US crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels last week, marking the seventh consecutive weekly decline.
Iran has shut the Strait of Hormuz after fresh US airstrikes, warning that any ship entering the waterway could be targeted. That’s a huge escalation in one of the world’s most critical oil routes.

Whats happening round the world (11 June 2026):

US–Iran conflict escalates

The United States has launched new strikes against targets linked to Iran, while Iran has announced measures affecting the strategically important Strait of Hormuz. The developments are raising concerns about regional stability and global energy markets.  

Oil prices and inflation concerns

Markets are reacting to the Middle East tensions, with higher energy prices contributing to renewed inflation concerns in several economies. Investors are watching for potential impacts on global growth.  

2026 FIFA World Cup begins

The expanded 48-team FIFA World Cup is starting across the United States, Canada, and Mexico, making it the largest World Cup in history.  

Violence and unrest in Belfast

Anti-immigrant violence and protests have continued in parts of Belfast following a recent knife attack, prompting appeals for calm from authorities and community leaders.  

OpenAI warns of Chinese influence operations

OpenAI has reported that Chinese propaganda and influence campaigns are being used to stir public debate and dissent around issues such as tariffs and data-center development.
Breaking: 🚨 Iran says it's fully closing the Strait of Hormuz after US strikes. This is a big deal for global oil shipping. https://t.co/hAvMPMfSRg

Prices were further supported by data from the US EIA showing that US crude inventories fell by 7.2M barrels in the week ended June 5, significantly exceeding analysts’ expectations of 4M barrels, as refiners moved to address supply gaps linked to the conflict.

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 11, 2026

Market Brief

National Development and Reform Commission: Accelerate construction of a modern industrial system and fully implement the “AI+” initiative.

China’s May CPI rose 1.2% year-over-year, while PPI increased 3.9%, hitting a 46-month high.

From January to May, taxpayers with overseas income paid approximately 13 billion yuan in back taxes.

Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission: Licensed firms may continue serving existing mainland clients, but cannot provide services within mainland China.

Middle East tensions escalate again as Trump threatens “very strong” strikes against Iran.

U.S. May CPI rose 4.2% year-over-year, the highest in three years.

Global equities fell broadly on Wednesday: All three U.S. major indexes dropped more than 1%, while South Korea’s market triggered circuit breakers for the third consecutive day.

International oil prices surged again, with U.S. crude and Brent crude both up over 3%, while gold prices continued to decline.

Top Stories

Middle East tensions have intensified again. U.S. President Trump threatened that, due to slow progress in negotiations to end the conflict with Iran, the United States will launch “very strong” strikes and may target Iranian infrastructure. U.S. Defense Secretary Hegseth stated that key Iranian facilities will be bombed. Iran’s armed forces vowed a “more severe, stronger, and more destructive” response. On June 10, explosions were reported across multiple locations in Iran, and the U.S. military announced a “defensive strike” in response to a downed military aircraft. Iran subsequently launched “intense” attacks on U.S. military bases in the Middle East.

The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission reiterated that licensed firms may continue serving existing mainland Chinese clients, including opening new accounts, provided that services are not delivered within mainland China and all relevant laws and regulations in Hong Kong and applicable jurisdictions are fully complied with. Hong Kong’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan said the regulatory actions target only non-compliant cross-border investment flows, and the central government continues to encourage compliant capital inflows into Hong Kong.

The U.S. Department of Labor released data showing that, driven by rising energy prices, U.S. CPI rose 4.2% year-over-year in May, the highest since May 2023 and in line with market expectations; core CPI rose 2.9% year-over-year, the highest since September 2025. U.S. CPI inflation returned to 4% for the first time in three years, further solidifying market expectations of a Federal Reserve rate hike this year.

China Macro & Policy

China’s National Bureau of Statistics released data showing that in May, the consumer market remained generally stable: CPI rose 1.2% year-over-year, matching April’s pace, while core CPI rose 1.1%. PPI rose 3.9% year-over-year, marking the third consecutive month of growth and the largest increase since August 2022.

The National Development and Reform Commission held a symposium with economic experts, stating it will fully utilize macro policies and leverage the combined effect of existing and new measures; accelerate the construction of a modern industrial system and fully implement the “AI+” initiative; crack down on “self-defeating” competition; and promptly study and prepare targeted, operational policy tools for timely implementation.

Vice Minister of China’s Ministry of Commerce and Deputy Chief Negotiator for International Trade Negotiations Ling Ji held talks with European Commission Director-General for Trade and Economic Security Jorgensen, extensively discussing preparations for the inaugural meeting of the China-EU Trade and Investment Consultation Mechanism. Both sides agreed to strive for tangible outcomes at the first meeting.

Data from China’s State Administration of Taxation showed that individual income tax revenue reached 764.39 billion yuan from January to May, up 12% year-over-year. Experts said the strong A-share market rally this year has driven rapid growth in personal income tax. Meanwhile, tax authorities have strengthened public awareness and policy guidance regarding overseas income, significantly improving taxpayer compliance: approximately 13 billion yuan in back taxes were collected from January to May.

The Ministry of Commerce, the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, and China State Railway Group issued a joint document titled “Several Measures to Promote Railway and Tourism Integration and Expand Service Consumption,” outlining 15 specific initiatives to accelerate railway infrastructure transformation for tourism, enrich railway tourism product offerings, and expand new tourism supply.

AI is reshaping the job market for humanities graduates. The topic “AI giants pay 30,000 yuan monthly to recruit humanities graduates” has trended on social media. Leading AI firms have increased their proportion of humanities roles from around 5% to 20–30%, creating new positions such as “AI narrative designer,” “large model humanities trainer,” and “AI ethics researcher,” with starting salaries typically ranging from 20,000 to 40,000 yuan per month.

China Equities Markets

A-shares edged lower: The SSE Index fell 0.42% to 3,993.23, the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 2.06%, the ChiNext Index slid 2.7%, and the Wind All China Index declined 1.27%, with market turnover reaching 2.64 trillion yuan. Semiconductor and computing hardware sectors diverged: semiconductor materials and PCB concepts rose, while optical communications and high-speed copper connections fell. Banking stocks and AI application names performed well, while power grid equipment and humanoid robotics stocks weakened.

The Hang Seng Index closed down 0.64% at 24,407.96, the Hang Seng Tech Index fell 0.94%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index dipped 0.07%. Electrical equipment, semiconductors, metals, coal, CPO, and AI application stocks declined, while consumer and biopharmaceutical stocks rose. Northbound funds net bought over 8.2 billion Hong Kong dollars; Tencent Holdings was added by around 2 billion Hong Kong dollars, while Alibaba was sold off by 2.3 billion Hong Kong dollars.

Taiwan’s Weighted Index closed down 3.31% at 43,225.54. AI-related stocks tumbled collectively: Delta Electronics fell nearly 9%, MediaTek and UMC dropped over 7%, Hon Hai Precision and ASE Technology Holdings each fell over 5%, and TSMC declined over 2%.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup USA-Mexico-Canada will officially kick off at 00:00 Beijing time on June 12 in Mexico City. As the first World Cup expanded to 48 teams, spanning 39 days and three countries, the tournament has ignited global fan enthusiasm and become a major opportunity for A-share companies across multiple sectors. Analysts said companies are already positioning themselves across the entire value chain—from sponsorship and IP merchandise to viewing consumption and technology enablement—to capitalize on this three-nation, 39-day “World Cup economy.”

China Industry News

China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology issued guidelines to promote innovation in “AI + information and communications,” targeting initial high-level autonomous networks by 2028 and significant breakthroughs in key technologies by 2030 to enhance integrated communication-sensing-computing-intelligence services.

Online rumors suggest a mandatory photovoltaic standard will be issued next week, covering polysilicon, wafers, and modules, potentially eliminating 30% of capacity based on previous estimates. Several companies responded that they are aware of the claims but cannot confirm their authenticity.

The China Association of Automobile Manufacturers reported that China’s automobile sales in May totaled 2.629 million units, down 2.1% year-over-year. New energy vehicle sales reached 1.496 million units, up 14.4% year-over-year, accounting for 56.9% of total new vehicle sales. NEV exports hit 446,000 units, up 110% year-over-year.

After two years of intense “full embrace of AI,” more companies are reassessing the real value of AI investments. Several tech giants have begun adjusting employee token allocation policies, moving away from uniform per-employee distribution toward dynamic allocation based on role and demand.

Rapid growth in commercial aerospace is drawing major solar energy firms into space-based energy. The “Space Energy Development Alliance” was recently launched, with founding members including GCL Group and Trina Solar. JA Solar, together with Satcon Technology and JEA, launched the “Space Energy Technology Ecosystem Alliance.”

A significant policy signal emerged to improve commuting efficiency within urban clusters. The Ministry of Transport and seven other departments jointly issued the “Implementation Plan for Enhancing Urban Cluster Commuting Efficiency (2026–2030),” aiming to establish one-hour commuting circles through rapid transit corridor construction.

The China Index Academy reported that in May, the planned construction area of residential land sold in 300 cities fell 27.1% year-over-year to 19.43 million square meters; land sale proceeds totaled 96.6 billion yuan, down 7.6% year-over-year but with a noticeably narrowed decline. Average land premium rates rose to 9.5%, supported by high-quality plots in core cities.

Fintech

The Financial Stability Board released a report stating that increasingly autonomous AI systems may heighten financial system risks and strongly encouraged institutions to implement protective measures—including against “agentic AI”—to mitigate these risks.

Hong Kong’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan said regulated stablecoins in Hong Kong could be launched as early as mid-year. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority recently hosted a corporate treasury and tokenization seminar to promote tokenization applications and encourage industry participation in its related projects.

SpaceX’s $75 billion IPO is imminent, with analysts warning the Bitcoin market could face liquidity outflows.

Corporate News & Updates

SpaceX will build a “Gigasat” super satellite factory in Texas to produce AI data center satellites in orbit, targeting 1 GW of space-based computing power by 2027 and 100 GW by 2030. SpaceX plans to launch an initial demonstration of its space AI computing infrastructure by the end of 2027, ahead of its IPO filing’s stated “earliest 2028” timeline.

Sources say OpenAI is negotiating to lease a planned 10-gigawatt data center campus in Ohio. The deal may include funding support from NVIDIA, with estimated construction costs of at least $500 billion. The first phase is expected to begin in 2028, with OpenAI potentially controlling the facility through a 20-year lease agreement.

Xiao Peng Group CEO He Xiaopeng announced in an internal memo that he is assuming the role of CEO of the robotics division, elevating humanoid robotics to the company’s highest strategic priority as it shifts from R&D to mass production and commercialization.

ByteDance’s DouBao officially launched a gray-scale test for its professional subscription tier, offering three plans: Standard, Enhanced, and Advanced Professional. The top-tier plan charges 500 yuan per month or 5,088 yuan annually for continuous subscription.

Xiaomi’s MiMo announced the official release and open-sourcing of MiMo Code V0.1, featuring the MiMo V2.5 multimodal model with a 1 million token context window, available free of charge for a limited time.

Moore Threads officially released and open-sourced MusaCoder, a specialized large language model for generating GPU kernel operations—the first open-source code model trained and validated entirely on domestic GPU infrastructure.

TSMC reported May revenue of 416.975 billion New Taiwan dollars, up 30.1% year-over-year; cumulative revenue for the first five months reached 1.96 trillion New Taiwan dollars, up 30% year-over-year. Analysts expect TSMC’s Q2 revenue growth to reach 35%.

Sources say Starbucks is evaluating options for its Japanese business, including equity sales and an IPO. Japan is one of Starbucks’ largest markets, with approximately 2,100 stores, most directly operated by the company.

Sources reveal that Lenovo finalized a key decision at its May internal meeting: starting in July, following the June 18 shopping festival, all product categories will be uniformly price-increased, with the magnitude matching the previous adjustment.

German humanoid robotics firm Neura Robotics announced a financing round of up to $14 billion, with investors including Tether, Qualcomm, Amazon, NVIDIA, Bosch, Schaeffler, and the European Investment Bank.

Global Macro & Policy

U.S. President Trump stated he does not intend to renew the USMCA trade agreement. On the topic of U.S. government equity stakes in AI firms, Trump said he will soon meet with 12 to 15 leading AI executives to discuss “giving something back to the public.”

The U.S. Treasury said net tariff revenue in May was approximately zero: $22 billion in tariffs collected matched $22 billion in refunds. Year-to-date fiscal revenue (since October 1, 2025) stands at $189 billion, up from $81 billion a year earlier. After calendar adjustments, the budget deficit for the first eight months of the fiscal year fell 2% year-over-year, or about $24 billion.

The Bank of Canada held its benchmark rate steady at 2.25%, in line with market expectations, marking its fifth consecutive hold. The bank warned that if Middle East tensions continue to push up energy prices and further elevate inflation, Canada may need to implement consecutive rate hikes.

The Bank of Japan announced that Governor Kuroda is hospitalized and will miss the monetary policy meeting on June 15–16. Markets widely expect a 25-basis-point rate hike next week; his absence is not expected to materially affect the decision.

Preliminary Bank of Japan data showed that Japan’s corporate goods price index rose 6.3% year-over-year in May, the highest since March 2023 and significantly above expectations. Due to yen depreciation, the yen-denominated import price index surged 25.5% year-over-year, the largest increase since November 2022.

Iran reported severe maritime losses due to war and related maritime restrictions: 360 vessels affected, of which 253 sank or were completely destroyed.

The European Union’s Copernicus Climate Change Service reported that May 2026 was the second-hottest May on record globally, with an anticipated El Niño event likely to trigger extreme weather across multiple regions in the coming months.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indexes closed lower: the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 1.87% to 49,918.78, the S&P 500 dropped 1.62% to 7,266.99, and the Nasdaq Composite declined 1.98% to 25,169.50. Caterpillar fell over 6%, Honeywell International dropped more than 4%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index slid 2.17%, with Tesla and NVIDIA each down over 3.5%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index fell 0.28%, while Bawang Cha Ji rose over 7% and GDS declined over 7%. Chip stocks extended recent declines: Micron and AMD both fell over 4%. U.S. May CPI rose 4.2%, the highest in three years.

European major indexes closed mixed: Germany’s DAX fell 0.97% to 24,195.31, France’s CAC 40 dropped 0.51% to 8,161.83, while the UK’s FTSE 100 rose 0.27% to 10,254.81. Escalating Middle East conflict and risks to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz pushed up oil prices; combined with rising expectations of ECB rate hikes, market risk aversion weighed on European equities.

Asia-Pacific indexes were mixed: South Korea’s market entered a “boom-bust” cycle. The KOSPI Composite Index closed down 4.52% at 7,730.82, triggering its third consecutive daily circuit breaker. The Nikkei 225 fell 1.89% to 64,179.27. Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, SoftBank Group, and Kioxia all tumbled sharply. India’s SENSEX rose 0.09% to 73,983.18.

The Korea Capital Market Institute’s president said South Korean retail investors have reached the maximum leverage limit set by local brokers, meaning they can no longer borrow additional funds. Data showed that, as of Tuesday’s close, the ratio of put-to-call open interest for the KOSPI 200 index stood at nearly 2.5 times, the highest in five years.

U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren urged the Securities and Exchange Commission to delay SpaceX’s record-breaking IPO, arguing that Wall Street’s enthusiasm could force index funds to passively buy SpaceX shares, exposing retail investors to risk. Warren also questioned SpaceX’s valuation.

Sources say SK Hynix plans to list in the U.S. as early as August, with SEC approval potentially granted during the week of June 22. Previous reports indicated the offering could raise up to $14 billion.

Oracle reported fourth-quarter fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS of $2.11 versus expectations of $1.97; adjusted revenue of $19.18 billion versus $19.09 billion expected.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market weakened in the afternoon, with yields on key government bonds rising, especially for maturities of five years and longer. Treasury futures closed lower: the 30-year contract fell 0.36%. The People’s Bank of China conducted 159 billion yuan in reverse repo operations, net injecting 159 billion yuan; liquidity conditions improved slightly.

The Ministry of Finance will auction 15 billion yuan of additional RMB-denominated treasury bonds in Hong Kong on June 16, including 4.5 billion yuan of 2-year, 4.5 billion yuan of 3-year, 3.5 billion yuan of 5-year, and 2.5 billion yuan of 10-year bonds.

U.S. Treasury yields rose across the board: 2-year yield up 2.72 bps to 4.139%, 3-year up 3.19 bps to 4.198%, 5-year up 3.72 bps to 4.278%, 10-year up 3.38 bps to 4.552%, and 30-year up 3.05 bps to 5.027%.

Commodities

The Dalian Commodity Exchange announced that, effective June 12, speculative and hedging margin requirements for coke futures will be lowered from 20% and 15% to 12%, respectively. Non-futures company members and clients may not open more than 500 contracts per day in any coke futures contract.

International precious metals prices fell broadly: COMEX gold futures dropped 4.49% to $4,094.10 per ounce; COMEX silver futures fell 2.67% to $63.50 per ounce. Although U.S. inflation data cooled slightly, escalating geopolitical tensions and sharply rising expectations for U.S. and European rate hikes increased real yields and holding costs, while a strong dollar added downward pressure.

U.S. crude oil futures rose 4.14% to $91.85 per barrel; Brent crude futures gained 3.88% to $95.00 per barrel. First, geopolitical risks: Trump warned of renewed strikes against Iran, raising fears over Strait of Hormuz shipping security and global supply disruptions. Second, inventory data: U.S. crude inventories fell more than expected, signaling tightening supply. Third, industry outlook: Shell’s CEO said global oil inventories are being depleted rapidly; even if Iran tensions end, prices will likely remain elevated.

Base metals on the LME all declined: LME tin fell 0.95% to $52,005 per ton, LME lead dropped 1.08% to $1,962.50, LME copper fell 1.22% to $13,449, LME aluminum declined 1.51% to $3,494, LME nickel slid 1.85% to $17,730, and LME zinc dropped 2.45% to $3,468.50.

FX & Currency Markets

On Wednesday, the onshore CNY closed at 6.7774 per USD, down 72 pips from the previous close, and settled at 6.7738 in the night session. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8130, up 17 pips from the prior day.

At New York’s close, the U.S. dollar index rose 0.09% to 100.04; most non-U.S. currencies fell: EUR/USD down 0.07% to 1.1536, GBP/USD down 0.08% to 1.3368, AUD/USD down 0.44% to 0.6997, USD/JPY up 0.12% to 160.5600, USD/CAD down 0.02% to 1.3944, USD/CHF up 0.22% to 0.8000, and offshore CNY fell 36 pips to 6.7820.

The Bank of Korea and financial regulators began a joint inspection of major foreign exchange banks to determine whether any institutions engaged in transactions that could destabilize the FX market; banks found in violation will face strict penalties. This is Korea’s first such inspection in 14 years.

Key Economic Events

OPEC releases monthly oil market report
The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be held from June 11 to July 19
[New Stock] Jinge Xincai officially lists on the Beijing Stock Exchange
[Earnings] Chow Tai Fook, Adobe

Oil prices climbed in early Asian trading after the U.S. launched strikes on Iranian military targets near the Strait of Hormuz. Brent rose above $92/bbl and WTI approached $89/bbl as markets weighed the risk of further escalation. Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories fell for an eighth straight week, adding to supply concerns.

Iran has announced the complete closure of the Strait of Hormuz and cancelled all previous exemptions for vessels.

Brent is up 2.72% to $97.07 on the escalation. US crude inventories fell 7.2 million barrels last week, the seventh consecutive weekly decline, ￼ as buyers replace supplies disrupted in the Gulf.

text: The physical reset lag is the part every ceasefire narrative misses. A VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier, the supertanker that moves most Gulf oil) repositioning from a Cape of Good Hope routing back to Hormuz adds 7-10 sailing days minimum — ships don't turn around on press releases. OPEC+ spare capacity announcements create paper barrels; physical flow depends on vessels staged in the right loading zone. Supply restoration is a logistics constraint, not a diplomatic one.
